Blocks.Text: IN THE UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
BEFORE THE HONORABLE LYDIA K. GRIGGSBY
So, if Mr. Goldstein was playing poker at the end of 2016,
but the loser did not pay him until 2017, that's accounted for
So the evidence is going to show that even if you know the
gambling wins -- that's what the government will talk about --
you have to know the losses. You have to know the shares. You
have to know when payment was received in order to be able to
accurately calculate how much a person owes on their gambling
taxes. And the evidence is going to show that the government
just doesn't have that right. They don't know what the correct
calculations were. They were never looking for the correct
calculations, and so the government has it wrong.
And the other thing you're going to hear about the 2016
tax return and the 2016 gambling taxes, is that the accountants
at GRF, the accounting firm, reached out to Mr. Goldstein at
the very last minute to put all of this together.
They may have also oversimplified what the process was.
You'll see the time that Mr. Goldstein needed to file his 2016
tax return, he was traveling all over the country and all over
the world for work. And so he did his best in good faith to
give his accountant the information that he asked for.
But it turns out the accountant didn't ask the right
questions, didn't put it down on the tax return correctly,
didn't go over the return with Mr. Goldstein before it was
filed. So a couple years later, when the government is talking
about supposedly false statements he made, you're going to see
how that stems from the errors from the accountant and them not
knowing how to properly record these taxes. The government's
investigation ignored all of this and it is not going to show
that Mr. Goldstein acted willfully to hide anything in 2016.
I want to talk about GRF, the accounting firm. This is
the next biggest set of charges that Mr. Goldstein faces. It's
accusations not that he filed a false tax return, that he aided
and assisted in the preparation of a false tax return.
And you're going to see how the errors on the tax returns
stem from the work of the accounting firm. Now, the evidence
shows that this charge makes no sense. Every year the
accountants at this accounting firm, GRF, prepared
It was their responsibility to ask for the documents that
they needed to prepare the taxes properly. And the way it
worked was they talked to Mr. Goldstein's office managers, who
are also the personal assistants. And, of course, if there
were questions that Mr. Goldstein needed to answer, he would
answer them and he would answer them accurately. But the
evidence is going to show there is a series of errors or
mistakes. This not a willful crime.
To understand how this happened, I want to just make sure
that you have a preview of how the law firm worked because
you're going to hear from these people at different points in
So Goldstein & Russell, small law firm, there are a
handful of attorneys. It is a collegial but informal
environment. They didn't have established systems for things
like invoices. And they were okay with that because it was
Now, instead of hiring an in-house accountant, again, it's
a small firm, they had firm managers who were also
Mr. Goldstein's personal assistants. And so they did mostly
administrative tasks, but they were the ones that communicated
with GRF, the accounting firm. And the bookkeeping was
outsourced to GRF. So they are the people who controlled the
accounting books. They are the people who also prepared
Not a single one of the people who worked at Goldstein &
Russell with Mr. Goldstein are going to say that he lied to
them about how to report a deduction or income, that he
falsified records, that he hid anything from them. He did not.
The other thing that you have to understand about
Goldstein & Russell is that, despite the name, Mr. Goldstein
was the only owner of this law firm. And what that means is
that he was entitled to all of the money in the bank accounts.
It wasn't illegal or a crime for him to be using firm money for
his personal payments. He was allowed to use all of the
